faqs.a: Self-sabotage usually isn't deliberate. It often grows out of fear, old beliefs about not deserving good things, or a wish to avoid the risk of trying and falling short. Backing out lowers the pressure in the moment, even when it works against what the person actually wants.

No. Self-sabotage isn't a clinical diagnosis. It's a way of describing a pattern of behavior. It can show up alongside conditions like depression or anxiety, but on its own it's a description, not a disorder.

There's no single fix, but noticing the pattern is a useful start. Many people find it helps to look at the fear underneath, ease up on harsh self-talk, and break goals into smaller steps. A therapist can help trace where the pattern comes from when it keeps blocking important goals.
